coredata.dc:title: Development of alginate film filled with halloysite-carbon dots for active food packaging
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Cinà, Giuseppe
Massaro, Marina
Cavallaro, Giuseppe
Lazzara, Giuseppe
Sánchez-Espejo, Rita
Viseras Iborra, César
D'Abrosca, Brigida
Fiorentino, Antonio
Messina, Grazia M.L.
Riela, Serena
coredata.dc:description: The development of functional bionanocomposites for active food packaging is of current interest to replace non-biodegradable plastic coatings. In the present work, we report the synthesis of an alginate-based nanocomposite filled with modified halloysite nanotubes (HNTs) to develop coatings with improved barrier properties for food packaging. Firstly, HNTs were chemically modified by the introduction of carbon dots units (CDs) onto their external surface (HNTs-CDs) obtaining a nanomaterial where CDs are uniformly present onto the tubes as verified by morphological investigations, with good UV absorption and antioxidant properties. Afterwards, these were dispersed in the alginate matrix to obtain the alginate/HNTs-CDs nanocomposite (Alg/HNTs-CDs) whose morphology was imaged by AFM measurements. The UV and water barrier properties (in terms of moisture content and water vapor permeability) were investigated, and the antioxidant properties were evaluated as well. To confer some antimicrobial properties to the final nanocomposite, the synthetized filler was loaded with a natural extract (E) from M. cisplatensis. Finally, the extract kinetic release both from the filler and from the nanocomposite was studied in a medium mimicking a food simulant and preliminary studies on the effect of Alg/HNTs-CDs/E on coated and uncoated fruits, specifically apples and bananas were also carried out.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Alginate
Modified halloysite
Nanocomposite
Active coating
Barrier properties

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: M. cisplatensis loading onto HNTs-CDs nanomaterial
Kinetic release of the extract from HNts-CDs/E nanomaterial
Preparation of the alginate nanocomposite films
Kinetic release of the extract from the Alg/HNTs-CDs/E film
Coating test on fruits: preliminary tests

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Schematic representation of the synthesis of HNTs-CDsa-c nanomaterials; (B) HAADF/STEM image of HNTs-CDsb nanomaterial; (CD) EDX elemental mapping images; (E) IC50 values of HNTs-CDsa-c (a = hexamethylenediamine, b = spermine and c = ethylenediamine) nanomaterials. Reported are mean values ± SD of two independent assays.

(a) Schematic representation of the synthesis of the Alg/HNTs-CDs films; Stress vs strain curves before and after the exposure to UV irradiation for films based on (b) Alg, (c) Alg/HNTs and (d) Alg/HNTs-CDs; (e) transmittance and (f) attenuation coefficient as function of the wavelength for Alg, Alg/HNTs and Alg/HNTs-CDs films Reported are the mean values of five (DMA) and three (UV) different experiments.
Tapping mode AFM images at different magnitude 5 μm, 2 μm and 3D image of (a-c) Alg; (s-f) Alg/HNTs; (g-i) Alg/HNTs-CDs.

(a-b) Hypothesized water barrier properties of (a) Alg and (b) Alg/HNTs-CDs films; (c) moisture content vs time of Alg, Alg/HNTs and Alg/HNTs-CDs films. Reported are the mean values of three different experiments. In each graph, different letters above the columns indicate significant differences (p < 0.05) in moisture content.
Kinetic release of E from (a) HNTs-CDs/E nanomaterial; (b) Alg/HNTs-CDs/E film in the food simulant at 25 °C. Reported are means values ± SD of two independent assays.
Fresh apple and banana slices uncoated and coated with Alg/HNTs-CDs/E nanocomposite film after 7 days of storage at room temperature, RH = 60 %.
Tensile properties of alginate-based films before and their exposure to UV irradiation. Reported are the mean values ± standard deviation of three different experiments.

Transmittance values of Alg/HNTs-CDs, Alg/HNTs and Alg nanocomposite films at 300 nm (UV-B) and 600 nm (visible light). Reported are the mean values of three independent measurements. Values are presented as mean ± standard deviation. Different letters in the same column indicate significantly different groups (p < 0.05) based on ANOVA analysis.
Root mean squared roughness (Rq), arithmetic roughness (Ra) and maximum roughness (Rmax) values for Alg, Alg/HNTs and Alg/HNTs-CDs.

Physical properties of Alg, Alg/HNTs and Alg/HNTs-CDs films. Reported are the mean values ± standard deviation of three different experiments. Values are presented as mean ± standard deviation. Different letters in the same column indicate significantly different groups (p < 0.05) based on ANOVA analysis.

ce:article-footnote.ce:note-para: The manuscript is dedicated to Prof. Maurizio Prato in occasion of his retirement.

head.ce:title: Development of alginate film filled with halloysite-carbon dots for active food packaging

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of functional bionanocomposites for active food packaging is of current interest to replace non-biodegradable plastic coatings. In the present work, we report the synthesis of an alginate-based nanocomposite filled with modified halloysite nanotubes (HNTs) to develop coatings with improved barrier properties for food packaging. Firstly, HNTs were chemically modified by the introduction of carbon dots units (CDs) onto their external surface (HNTs-CDs) obtaining a nanomaterial where CDs are uniformly present onto the tubes as verified by morphological investigations, with good UV absorption and antioxidant properties. Afterwards, these were dispersed in the alginate matrix to obtain the alginate/HNTs-CDs nanocomposite (Alg/HNTs-CDs) whose morphology was imaged by AFM measurements. The UV and water barrier properties (in terms of moisture content and water vapor permeability) were investigated, and the antioxidant properties were evaluated as well. To confer some antimicrobial properties to the final nanocomposite, the synthetized filler was loaded with a natural extract (E) from M. cisplatensis. Finally, the extract kinetic release both from the filler and from the nanocomposite was studied in a medium mimicking a food simulant and preliminary studies on the effect of Alg/HNTs-CDs/E on coated and uncoated fruits, specifically apples and bananas were also carried out.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, due to the growing of urbanized population, there has been an increase in food demand that requires efficient food packaging strategies to preserve food quality as much as possible [1,2]. Historically, plastic-based materials have been used for these purposes; however, after their use, they were often disposed of without any reuse.

Since 2018, when European Commission approved a directive to prohibit or reduce the single use of plastic packaging, attention has shifted to biobased and/or biodegradable materials such as natural biopolymers as chitosan, hydroxypropyl cellulose, and alginate [3].

Alginate (Alg) is a linear and biodegradable polyuronic acid constituted by α-L-guluronate and β-D-mannuronic units [4]. It is promising for application in the food packaging field because of its interesting physicochemical features such as good oxygen barrier, good compatibility and biodegradability, low cost, low toxicity, and film-forming ability [5]. However, its extensive use is limited by its low water and poor ultraviolet light barrier abilities, and lack of antioxidant and antimicrobial activities.
To overcome these drawbacks, a common strategy is that to introduce a suitable filler into the polymeric matrix for developing functional nanocomposites [6–8].
Among the different fillers that can be used, halloysite represents a green and low-cost alternative to reinforce polymeric matrices [9,10].

Halloysite, is a naturally occurring aluminosilicate clay mineral, belonging to kaolin group, with the chemical formula of Al2Si2O5(OH)4 × nH2O. Chemically it is constituted by rolling kaolinite sheets where siloxane groups are exposed on the external surface, while aluminol groups are present in the lumen and because of this, its morphology is prevalently tubular, so commonly it is usually referred to it as halloysite nanotubes (HNTs). In the HNTs, the possibility to selectively modify the tubes outer and inner surfaces, allows to obtain nanostructures with enhanced interfacial properties [11–13].

HNTs can indeed form strong hydrogen bonds with polymer and the interface bonding can increase after covalent modification of HNTs external surface [14]. In this context, organosilane modification has received much attention as a tool for improving polymer–filler affinity. For example Ning et al. found that the mechanical performance of polypropylene reinforced with modified HNTs increases [15], while Guo et al. reported that the introduction of 3-(trimethoxysilyl)propyl methacrylate onto HNTs external surface enhanced the physicochemical properties of polyamide 6 [16]. In addition, the presence of an empty lumen with a diameter of ca. 15 nm, is a very important feature since it allows the encapsulation of biologically active substances such as antioxidants, antimicrobial, essential oils and so on [12,17–23]. Due to these peculiarities, HNTs have been widely used as filler for the development of coatings for food packaging applications [24–26]. The introduction of HNTs into polymeric matrices provides different advantages [27–29]. For example, it has been demonstrated that the presence of the clay in the chitosan matrix enhances the water barrier properties of the resulting nanocomposite in comparison to the pristine one [30].

Herein, we report the synthesis of an alginate-based nanocomposite reinforced with modified HNTs for food packaging applications. To ensure good barrier properties, the HNTs filler was first covalently modified on the external surface by introducing carbon dots (CDs) units, obtaining the HNTs-CDs nanomaterials. CDs were used since it is known that they can confer, when used as filler for packaging materials, antioxidant, and antimicrobial properties to the final nanocomposite [31]. The use of HNTs-CDs filler, in comparison to the pristine clay, could also enhance the UV and water barrier properties of the resulting film.

Once synthetized and characterized, the HNTs-CDs filler was dispersed into the alginate matrix to obtain the Alg/HNTs-CDs films. The barrier properties of the nanocomposite, in terms of water vapor permeability and moisture content, were investigated, and its UV shielding ability was evaluated as well by studying the mechanical properties before and after irradiation under UV light. The morphology of the nanocomposite was investigated by atomic force microscopy (AFM) measurements. Furthermore, to develop functional nanocomposites for active food packaging, the Alg/HNTs-CDs was loaded with a natural extract (E) from M. cisplatensis leaves, which has shown promising in vitro antimicrobial activity [32]. Kinetic release experiments were performed in food simulant D1, to assess the release capacity of the nanocomposite, and its antioxidant properties were determined by DPPH method.

Finally, preliminary studies of the effect of Alg/HNTs-CDs/E nanocomposite film on coated and uncoated fruits were carried out by visually monitoring the change in the appearance of apples and bananas, chosen as models for food with high content of polyphenolic compounds.

Firstly, the HNT-CDs fillers were synthesized following a procedure reported previously by us [33,34]. To find the best experimental conditions for obtaining fluorescent and antioxidant nanomaterials for use in the food packaging field, L-tartaric acid was chosen as carbonaceous source and three different amines were used as passivant agent. Briefly, L-tartaric acid modified halloysite (HNTs-1) was reacted with hexamethylenediamine (a), spermine (b) or ethylene diamine (c) by MW mediated pyrolysis reaction (Fig. 1) yielding HNTs-CDsa-c. The nanomaterials show a loading percent of organic matter compared to the precursor of ca. 8.4, 4.8 and 3.8 wt%, for HNTs-CDsa, HNTs-CDsb and HNTs-CDsc, respectively, as estimated by thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) (Fig. S.1). The presence of CDs onto HNTs external surface was verified by FT-IR spectroscopy, which showed that all the obtained nanomaterials possess several oxygen and nitrogen functional groups on the particle surface with different contents, according to the amines used (Fig. S.1).

Morphological investigation by HAADF/STEM showed that the introduction of CDs onto HNTs external surface led to particle aggregation probably due to the existence of some attractive interactions among the different CDs units present at the external surface (Fig. 1A) [33]. Energy Dispersive X-ray measurements (EDX) show the presence of C and N atoms related to the organic portions in addition to the typical elements of the clay, namely, Si, Al and O atoms (Fig. 1C). From elemental mapping extrapolated from EDX it is possible to conclude that the CD units are uniformly distributed onto HNTs as highlighted by the spots related to the C and N atoms (Fig. 1B). The relative amount of these elements was quantified by XPS measurements (Table S.1).

The HNTs-CDsa-c nanomaterials showed excellent UV absorption capabilities and wavelength dependent emission properties due to the presence of small and uniform in size CDs on their external surface as proved by spectroscopic measurements (Figs. S.2 and 3).

The aqueous mobility of all synthetized nanomaterials was investigated by Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) measurements. It was found that the introduction of carboxylic acids in HNTs-1 nanomaterial, in comparison to pristine HNTs, slows the aqueous diffusion of the HNTs as shown by the increased values of apparent hydrodynamic diameter (514 ± 36 and 295 ± 16 nm, for HNTs-1 and HNTs, respectively). A similar trend was observed for the apparent hydrodynamic diameter values of HNTs-CDsa-c nanomaterials (523 ± 44, 619 ± 11 and 664 ± 6 nm, for HNTs-CDsa, HNTs-CDsb and HNTs-CDsc, respectively). These results might be due to electrostatic attraction interactions occurring between the organic moieties covalently linked onto the outer surface of halloysite. Similar observations were found for halloysite nanotubes with a selective modification of the external surface by exploiting both covalent and supramolecular interactions [33]. The presence of carboxylic groups slightly enhances the negative charge of pristine HNTs as evidenced by the ζ-potential data (−20.0 ± 5.0 and − 18.0 ± 1.0 mV, for HNTs-1 and HNTs, respectively). After the pyrolysis process, different ζ-potential values were obtained, indicating that a modification of HNTs external surface occurred (−18.0 ± 1.3, −15.7 ± 0.8 and − 22.2 ± 1.0 mV, for HNTs-CDsa, HNTs-CDsb and HNTs-CDsc, respectively).

To assess if all synthetized HNTs-CDs nanomaterials showed any antioxidant properties, their radical scavenging activity was evaluated by using the DPPH method. The antioxidant capacity of the HNT-CDs nanomaterials in MeOH was found to be in the range 0–2.5 mg mL−1. The HNTs-CDsa-c nanomaterials showed good antioxidant activity that increases with their concentration (IC50 in the range 0.8–4 mg mL−1); depending on the amines used. In particular, the best antioxidant activity was observed for HNTs-CD1a nanomaterial whereas the worst was observed for HNTs-CD1b (Fig. 1E). It is known that the antioxidant activity of CDs arises from the functional groups present on their surface, such as carboxyl or amino groups.

Since the DPPH radical (DPPH·) can be quenched by hydrogen atom or electron transfers, or by a combination of the two processes, thus, it is possible to hypothesize that the -COOH groups present at the CDs surface, in HNTs-CDs, allow a hydrogen transfer from the CDs surface to the DPPH· converting it into DPPH-H. On the contrary, it is reported that the primary amino groups act by an indirect hydrogen transfer mechanism. Specifically, a fast electron transfer between the -NH2 groups and the DPPH· occurs, forming an aminium cation radical/DPPH· anion complex, which undergoes deprotonation to give DPPH-H [35,36].
Furthermore, as already reported, the HNTs-CDs based nanomaterials showed to be highly biocompatible in a wide concentration range up to 30 mg mL−1 [37].
Thus, the presence of different amino and carboxylic groups onto HNTs-CDs surface could improve, the interfacial properties in the final nanocomposite when used as filler for polymeric matrices.

In light of these findings, the HNTs-CDsa nanomaterial, hereinafter called HNTs-CDs, was used as a filler for alginate biopolymer to obtain an Alg/HNTs-CDs film. As observed in Fig. 2a, a homogeneous and colorless nanocomposite was obtained by a casting method.
The physico-chemical properties of the Alg/HNTs-CDs film, in terms of stability to UV irradiation and barrier properties were investigated to evaluate its potential use in food packaging.

Exposure of food to natural or artificial light during the supply chain is, indeed, an important factor that affects the quality of the final product, as it degrades food through different photooxidation and photodegradation reactions [38]. Therefore, more and more researchers are focusing on the development of UV-blocking materials that could prevent food spoilage. In this context, CDs exhibit good scattering and adsorption properties that allow them to convert UV photons into heat, thereby protecting food from UV irradiation [31].

In light of these premises, we investigated the influence of UV radiation exposure on the tensile performances of alginate films filled with both pristine HNTs and HNTs-CDs nanomaterials. In this regard, Fig. 2b-d shows the stress vs strain curves of Alg/HNTs-CDs, Alg/HNTs and Alg nanocomposites before and after their exposure to UV irradiation. The analysis of the curves allowed us to determine the tensile properties of the films in terms of elastic modulus, stress at breaking point and ultimate elongation (Table 1 ).

As a general consideration, we observed that UV irradiation decreases the ultimate elongation. Accordingly, an increase of the elastic modulus was detected for all films after their UV exposure highlighting an enhancement of the rigidity. The mechanical resistance of the films was evaluated by considering the stress at break values. Table 1 shows that the sample based on pristine alginate exhibits lower mechanical resistance after UV exposure as highlighted by the relevant reduction (28 %) of the stress at break. This effect was slightly attenuated by filling alginate with neat HNTs. In particular, we calculated a decrease of 21 % for Alg/HNTs film exposed to UV radiation. According to literature [39–41], HNTs can generate an enhancement of alginate film guaranteeing a partial protection of the biopolymer structure from UV exposure. Remarkably, we observed that the stress at break is unaltered by UV for Alg/HNTs-CDs sample. This finding highlights that alginate is totally protected by the addition of HNTs-CDs. Namely, we can state that the HNTs functionalized with CDs represent an efficient photo-protective barrier that preserves the structure of alginate matrix from UV exposure.

The light transmittance of a material is an important parameter to consider when it is used in food packaging, as it influences the attractiveness and appearance of the nanocomposite films, as well as the capacity to protect products from degradation due to light exposure [42].

In Fig. 2e the transmission spectra of pristine Alg, Alg/HNTs and Alg/HNTs-CDs nanocomposites are reported. As a general result, transmittance increases with the wavelength, in agreement with the light scattering determined by the particles. As expected, the introduction of HNTs or HNTs-CDs led to a decrease in transmittance values, which is more pronounced in the case of HNTs-CDs. To investigate the effects of the filler on the transparency of the alginate nanocomposite, the attenuation coefficient (k) was calculated, and the results, as a function of the wavelength, are shown in Fig. 2f.

The k values were calculated by the absorbance (A) data using the following equation:
(1)
k =
A
2.3 × D
where D represents the thickness of the film (m).

As it is possible to observe from Fig. 2f, the introduction of the inorganic filler enhanced the attenuation coefficient of alginate, thus, the nanocomposites exhibited a lower transparency compared to the film based on the pristine Alg [43].

Table 2 shows the transmittance values of Alg/HNTs-CDs, Alg/HNTs and Alg films at 300 nm (UV-B) and 600 nm (visible light). As it is possible to observe, both nanocomposite films (Alg/HNTs and Alg/HNTs-CDs) show a reduction in the transmittance value at 300 nm in comparison to pristine Alg (p < 0.05), and specifically, the Alg/HNTs-CDs nanocomposite presents the lowest value. This finding is likely due to the presence of CDs in the HNTs-CDs filler, which possess strong absorption in the 200–300 nm wavelength range. Thus, the introduction of HNTs-CDs filler into the alginate matrix improves the UV-B light absorption, resulting in a promising UV-barrier nanocomposite that can prolong food product shelf life.

Fig. 6 shows the morphology of Alg/HNTs-CDs, Alg/HNTs and Alg films at different magnitude, in particular 5 μm and 2 μm, moreover a 3D image is reported to better highlight the differences in their surface topography.

Alg films form a homogeneous layer characterized by a quite compact network with high cross-linking density (Fig. 3a-c). The addition of HNTs seems to have an effect on the direction of the films, in particular it is slightly tilted with respect to the acquisition direction of the images (Fig. 3d-f), though the film roughness remains mostly unchanged. It is worth of note that on the surface of Alg/HNTs-CDs films (Fig. 3 g-i), the CDs are clearly visible, indicating a segregation process at the interface, (see in particular Fig. 3i). We can argue this behavior in terms of electrostatic interaction, in fact the Alg is negatively charged, as well as the HNTs-CDs nanomaterial, so the repulsive forces between them drive the CDs to migrate at the interface. By section analysis it is possible to measure the CDs dimensions that result to be about 5.43 ± 1.24 nm, that approximately correspond to the size of CDs present at HNTs surface [33].
The morphological differences between the films are quantified by the change of roughness values reported in Table 3 .

In Table 4 the physical properties of the obtained films and for comparison those of pristine Alg one, are reported. As it is possible to observe, the introduction of pristine HNTs did not lead to any variation in the thickness of the resulting Alg/HNTs film, whereas the addition of 5 wt% of HNTs-CDs nanomaterial increased the thickness of the Alg/HNTs-CDs film. This finding could be due to the existence of some interactions between HNTs-CDs filler and the alginate matrix or among the different HNTs-CDs units dispersed in the polymer. The introduction of both fillers, namely pristine HNTs and HNTs-CDs, significantly affects both the moisture content and the water vapor permeability (WVP) (p < 0.05). These two factors are very important parameters for food packaging application since they can affect the quality and integrity of the food [44].

In absence of any filler, the environment moisture is transferred to the food by diffusing through the polymeric matrix (Fig. 4a). When the clay is introduced as filler, it decreases the WVP of the nanocomposite films, because, as it is known [42], halloysite possesses water barrier properties creating tortuous pathways for the diffusion of water molecules and thereby increasing their average path lengths through the film matrix. As reported in the case of montmorillonite, this could be ascribed to the impermeable silicate layer of the clay which hinders the water diffusion [45]. The introduction of HNTs-CDs in Alg/HNTs-CDs led a further decrease in the WVP value in comparison to both pristine Alg and Alg/HNTs films. This is attributable to the presence of CDs on the halloysite external surface. CDs, indeed, as already stated [46] have an external surface rich in amino and carboxylic acid groups that could establish electrostatic attraction or hydrogen bonding interactions with the -COO− and -OH groups of the alginate matrix, further hindering the interaction of the polymer with water molecules (Fig. 4b).

Similar considerations can be made for the moisture content of the nanocomposite films. Also in this case, the introduction of HNTs in the alginate matrix decreases the moisture content (MC) of the nanocomposite due to their water barrier properties. The MC value further decreases in the presence of HNTs-CDs filler, in agreement with the WVP findings (Fig. 4c). It is noteworthy that by collecting the MC value over the time, the introduction of HNTs or HNTs-CDs nanomaterial decreases the moisture content after ca. 72 h, whereas in pristine Alg it continues to increase even after 7 days. Also in this case, indeed, the presence of CDs is crucial to crosslink the alginate matrix by the presence of favorable interactions between the polymer and CDs on HNTs surface, limiting the water uptake.

HNTs-CDs nanomaterial showed good antioxidant properties with a low IC50 (0.8 mg mL−1) because of the presence of different amino and carboxylic groups onto its surface [35], making it a suitable filler for developing nanocomposites for active food packaging. Antioxidant packaging materials can indeed increase shelf life, minimizing oxidation, and preventing microbial spoilage. The antioxidant activity of the developed Alg/HNTs-CDs film was assessed using the DPPH method, with 50 % v/v water/ethanol as the solvent chosen as simulant for food with lipophilic character (D1) according to the European Regulations [47]. In this solvent mixture the HNTs-CDs nanomaterial showed good antioxidant activity that increased with its concentration (IC50 of 1.0 mg mL−1). By applying the same method to the Alg/HNTs-CDs film, it was found that the DDPH inhibition percentage after 96 h was ca. 25 %, indicating that it retains the antioxidant properties of the filler, which can be beneficial for future packaging applications.

To develop functional nanocomposites for active food packaging [48], the Alg/HNTs-CDs film was loaded with a natural extract that has shown promising antibacterial activity [32]. For this purpose, the methanolic extract of M. cisplatensis leaves was selected, which showed an IC50 of 128 μg mL−1 and 64 mg mL−1 on two different Staphylococcus aureus strain (ATC29213 and MRSA, respectively) [32].

The HNTs-CDs nanomaterial, with halloysite modified on the external surface, still possesses an empty lumen that can be used for the loading of hydrophobic molecules [49]. Thus, based on the physico-chemical properties of both HNTs and M. cisplatensis components (p-coumaroyl alkylphloroglucinols, coumarin derivatives and endoperoxide G3), interaction between them can occur via hydrophobic effects, electrostatic attraction, and hydrogen bonding interaction occurring between the Al-OH groups present at the lumen of HNTs and the carbonyl, hydroxyl and oxygen groups of the extract components [49].

Using a common strategy for loading active species inside HNTs carriers [50], the extract (E) was loaded onto the HNTs-CDs nanomaterial. After work-up, the nanomaterial showed a drug loading of ca. 6.8 wt% as highlighted by UV–vis measurements. Afterwards, the loaded HNTs-CDs/E was used as filler for the synthesis of the Alg/HNTs-CDs/E film. To avoid degradation and volatilization of the extract components, instead of using the “classic” casting method for film preparation, an easy and cheap casting method under reduced pressure, previously adopted by us for similar systems, was used [51].

Thus, the kinetic release of the extract for both HNTs-CDs/E nanomaterial and Alg/HNTs-CDs/E film was evaluated in the food simulant at 25 °C using UV–vis measurements, assuming that at each time all extract components were proportionally released [49].

In Fig. 5a the kinetic release of the extract from the HNTs-CDs/E nanomaterial was reported. In this case, the common adopted dialysis bag method was used. As it is possible to note, the total amount of extract loaded into the nanomaterial was released within 250 min and the kinetic data were well fitted by a first order model (M∞ = 98.8 ± 3.6 wt%; k = 0.013 ± 0.001 min−1, R2 = 0.9922). Conversely, Fig. 5b shows the release profile (expressed as mg of E per cm2 of film), which was successfully described by the Power Fit model (k = 0.007 ± 0.001 h−1, n = 0.11, R2 = 0.9860). These findings indicate that the introduction of HNTs-CDs/E filler in the Alg film, could provide a slow and sustained release of the antimicrobial species, ensuring long-term food protection [52]. These results agree with other reported in literature about the release of orange and thyme EO-loaded HNTs from multilayer polyamide and polyolefin films [53], Zataria multiflora essential oil loaded halloysite nanotubes as filler for nanocellulose sachet [54], carvacrol-loaded HNTs from zein films [19], and oregano essential oil encapsulated in HNTs [18].

Preliminary studies of the effect of Alg/HNTs-CDs/E film on coated and uncoated fruits were carried out by visually following the change in the appearance of apples and bananas, chosen as models. Fig. 6 shows pictures of uncoated and coated apple and banana slices with the developed film after 7 days of storage at room temperature, relative humidity (RH) = 60 %. Both uncoated fruits show an increasing browning during the storage period due to some oxidation phenomena, furthermore, in the case of uncoated apple, the occurrence of some microbial degradation, after 7 days of storage occurs.
On the contrary, the coating with Alg/HNTs-CDs/E film prevents oxidation and enhances the storage time of the fruits.

Therefore, from these preliminary investigations, it could be possible to conclude that the combination of alginate biopolymer with HNTs-CDs filler and the M. cisplatensis extract could provide an efficient UV barrier, preventing oxidation, and providing antimicrobial protection, thus extending the shelf-life of fruits.
All reagents used for nanomaterial synthesis were purchased from Merck and used without further purification. M. cisplatensis extract was obtained as reported in literature [32].
Microwave-assisted syntheses were carried out using a CEM DISCOVER monomode system in a closed vessel.
FT-IR spectra (KBr) were recorded using an Agilent Technologies Cary 630 FT-IR spectrometer. Specimens for these measurements were prepared by mixing 5 mg of the sample powder with 100 mg of KBr.

Thermogravimetry analyses were carried out by means of a Q5000 IR apparatus (TA Instruments). The measurements were conducted under nitrogen atmosphere (the gas flows were set at 25 and 10 cm3 min−1 for the sample and the balance, respectively) from 25 to 700 °C. The heating rate was set at 20 °C min−1.
ζ-Potential and dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements were performed by using a Zetasizer Nano-ZS (Malvern Instruments) at 25.0 ± 0.1 °C.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed with an FEI Titan G2 60–300 ultra-high resolution transmission electron microscope (FEI, Lausanne, Switzerland) coupled with analytical electron microscopy (AEM) performed with an energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (XEDS) detector. AEM spectra were saved in scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM) mode with a high angle annular dark field (HAADF) detector. Elemental maps were also collected using X-rays.
UV–vis measurements were made with a Beckmann DU 650 spectrometer. Spectra were acquired between 250 and 700 nm.

Fluorescence measurements both in solution and in solid state were performed with a JASCO FP8300 spectrofluorometer. For measurements in solution the excitation and emission slits were set at 5 nm and spectra were acquired in wavelength interval ranging between 300 and 700 nm. Solid state spectra were acquired by setting the excitation and emission slits at 5 and 2.5 nm, respectively in the wavelength interval ranging between 300 and 700 nm.

Tensile properties were determined by means of a DMA Q800 instrument (TA Instruments). For all mechanical measurements, the samples were films of rectangular shape (10.00 mm × 6.00 mm × 0.120 mm), while tensile tests were performed with a stress ramp of 1 MPa min−1 at 26.0 ± 0.5 °C. We determined the values of the elastic modulus, which was calculated by the slope of the linear region in the stress vs strain curve. In addition, we estimated the tensile strength (defined as the tensile stress at which the material fractures), and the elongation at the breaking point. Each nanocomposite was measured five times, and the average values are reported.
UV–vis measurements were made with a Beckmann DU 650 (Beckmann, Fullerton, CA, USA) spectrometer.

Tartaric acid (460 mg, 10 equiv.) and 1-Ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC, 62 mg, 10 equiv.) were suspended in anhydrous DMF (15 mL). The suspension was left to stir at room temperature for 10 min. Then 500 mg of HNTs-NH2 were quickly added. The mixture was left under stirring for 48 h. After this time, the solvent was filtered off, and the powder was rinsed with H2O CH2Cl2 and finally dried at 80 °C under vacuum.

HNT-CDsa-c nanomaterials were obtained upon microwave irradiation of an aqueous dispersion of HNTs-1 in the presence of ethylenediamine (EDA), spermine or hexamethylene diamine (1:1 mol mol−1). Typically, halloysite based nanomaterials (100 mg), EDA (150 μL) hexamethylenediamine (50 mg) or spermine (50 mg) and water (460 μL) were heated at 240 °C, at 200 W (the set value) for 3 min in the presence of a catalytic amount of TEA. In the process of microwave heating, the solution changes color from white to yellow as a result of formation of CDs on the halloysite external surface. After this, the solid powder was collected, rinsed several times with ethanol, to remove the unreacted reagents and dried at 80 °C under vacuum.

Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) measurements were carried out in tapping mode by using a Nanoscope IIIA-MultiMode AFM (Digital Instruments, Santa Barbara, CA, USA). The device was equipped with a < J > calibrated scanner using grating manufacturers. Images were recorded at scan rate of 1 Hz and 512 × 512 pixels per image (i.e., in high resolution conditions) by using 0.5–2 Ω·cm Phosphorous (n) doped silicon tips mounted on cantilevers with a nominal force constant of 40 N/m and a resonant frequency of 300 kHz. The force was maintained at the lowest possible value by continuous adjusting the set point during imaging. Image analysis was carried out using DI software, version 4.23r6. The images were flattened to remove background slopes.

Film roughness was measured on TM images obtained at a scan speed of 1 Hz over scanned areas of 2 × 2 μm2 for a minimum of six separate zones for each sample obtained from different regions. The roughness values are expressed in terms of Ra, Rq and Rmax. In particular, Ra expresses the arithmetic average of the absolute values of the surface height deviations measured from the mean plane within the cursor box using
R a
=
1 n
∑
j = 1
n
Z j
. Rq is the standard deviations of the Z values and is calculated as
R q
=
∑
Z i
2
n
where the current Z value and n is the number of points within the cursor box. Finally, Rmax is the maximum vertical distance between the highest and lowest data points within the cursor box.

ce:section.ce:section-title: M. cisplatensis loading onto HNTs-CDs nanomaterial
ce:section.ce:para:
To a dispersion of HNTs-CDs (50 mg) in H2O (5 mL), 0.5 mL of a 0.45 mg mL−1 solution of E in MeOH was added. The suspension was sonicated for 5 min and then evacuated for 3 cycles. The suspension was left under agitation for 6 h at room temperature. After this time, the powder was washed several times with water to remove unreacted E was removed and dried under vacuum. The amount of E loaded on HNTs-CDs was estimated by UV–vis spectroscopy by analyzing the filtrate solution at the wavelength of 276 nm using a calibration curve (see SI). The loading percent (LD%) was calculated by the following equation:
(2)
LD % =
E HNTs
m HNTs
+
E HNTs
× 100
where EHNTs is the amount of M. cisplatensins extract loaded on HNTs and mHNTs is the amount of halloysite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Kinetic release of the extract from HNts-CDs/E nanomaterial
ce:section.ce:para: The release of E from HNTs-CDs/E nanomaterial was performed as follows: 10 mg of HNTs-CDs/E filler were transferred into a dialysis membrane (Medicell International Ltd. MWCO 12–14,000 with a diameter of 21.5 mm) and wet with 1 mL of the food simulant D1 (H2O/Ethanol 1:1 v/v). The membrane was then put in a round bottom flask containing 9 mL of food simulant at 25 °C under constant stirring. At fixed times, 1 mL of the release medium was withdrawn and analyzed. To keep constant the volume of the release medium 1 mL of release medium was added each time to replace the withdrawn one. The extract concentration in the solution was determined by UV–vis spectrophotometry using the Lambert-Beer law, measuring the absorbance at 276 nm, by assuming that at each time all extract components were proportionally released.

Total amount of extract released (Ft) was calculated as follows:
(3)
F t
=
V m
C t
+
∑
t = 0
t − 1
V a
C i
where V
m and C
t are the volume and concentration of extract at time t. V
a is the volume of the sample and C
i is extract’ concentration (mg mL−1) at time i (i < t).

Kinetic data were analyzed by the following mathematical models:
(4)
First order
F t
=
M ∞
∙
1 −
e
− kt
(5)
Power
Fit
F t
=
kt n
where Ft and M∞ are the amount of extract released at time t and infinite, respectively; k is the release constant, and n is the release exponent, depending on the release mechanism and the geometry of the device.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the alginate nanocomposite films
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, a 2 wt% aqueous sodium alginate solution was prepared, and it was left to stir at room temperature overnight. Then, 10 mg of HNTs, HNT-CDs or HNTs-CDs/E were added to the alginate solution and kept under stirring for ca. 6 h. For the Alg/HNTs and Alg/HNTs-CDs nanocomposite film, the well-dispersed aqueous mixture was poured into glass Petri dishes at 60 °C to evaporate water until the weight was constant. Dried nanocomposite films contained 5 wt% of filler. Conversely, the Alg/HNTs-CDs/E nanocomposite film was prepared by pouring the well-dispersed aqueous mixture into glass Petri and then it was left at room temperature and under vacuum in a chamber in the presence of silica and P2O5, which were freshly added during experiment.

The moisture content (MC) of the nanocomposite films was measured on 3 specimens of 2 × 2 cm piece of film. Samples were first dried under vacuum at 25 °C for ca. 2 h. Afterwards, the films were placed in hermetic chambers containing oversaturated NaBr solution to maintain the relative humidity (RH) at a constant level of 60 %. The temperature of hermetic chambers was set at 25.0 ± 0.5 °C. The films were weighted each 24 h for one week. Moisture content was calculated according to the equation:
(6)
MC % = 100 ×
W f
−
W i
W f
where MC% is the percentage of the moisture content expressed as average ± standard deviation of the 3 specimens. Wf and Wi are the weights after and before exposure to 60 % RH.

The water vapor transmission rate (WVTR) was determined according to ASTM E96–05 (ASTM, 2005). Alg, Alg/HNTs and Alg/HNTs-CDs nanocomposite films were sealed on a glass vial containing CaCl2. The so constituted vials were placed inside a vacuum chamber in the presence of supersatured NaBr solution, RH 60 % at 25 °C. The nanocomposite films were weighed after 24 h and the WVTR was calculated as follows:
(7)
WVTR =
∆ m
t
A
where ∆m/t is the slope of the straight line of the graph plotted of weight gained (g) vs. time (h) and A is area of the vials (m2).

The WVP was calculated from Eq. 4:
(8)
WVP = WVTR ×
L
P
∆ RH
where L is the thickness of film (m), P is the saturation vapor pressure of water at 25 °C, and ΔRH is relative humidity difference between the two sides of vials.

Antioxidant activity of HNTs-CDs nanomaterials and Alg/HNTs-CDs nanocomposite film was determined by using the 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl radical (DPPH⋅) method. The radical scavenging activity was determined as follows.

Several aliquots of the HNTs-CDs nanomaterial were dispersed in 1 mL of EtOH 50 %(v/v) (concentration ranging from 0 to 4.3 mg mL−1) and to the obtained dispersions 1 mL of DPPH solution (1.5 × 10−4 M) in EtOH 50 %(v/v) was added. The mixture was then vigorously mixed and allowed to stand at room temperature in the dark for 40 min. The inhibition of free radical DPPH in percent was calculated as:
(9)
Inhibition
%
=
Abs 0
−
Abs s
Abs 0
× 100
where Abs0 is the absorbance of the DDPH solution without HNTs-CDs ( λ = 523 nm), and Abss is the absorbance of the test sample. All tests were carried out in duplicate, and inhibition percentages were reported as mean ± SD.
The percentage of inhibition of released HNTs-CDs was determined after immersion for 96 h of a 1 ×1 cm sample of the Alg/HNTs-CDs nanocomposite film in the food simulant D1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Kinetic release of the extract from the Alg/HNTs-CDs/E film
ce:section.ce:para: For the kinetic release of extract from Alg/HNTs-CDs/E film, 1 × 1 cm film samples were immersed in 1 mL of food simulant in different sealed vials and stored at 25 °C. At various selected times, films were removed from the sample and the liquid was subjected to UV–vis analysis to determine the amount of extract released.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Coating test on fruits: preliminary tests
ce:section.ce:para: Coating tests on apple and banana were carried out. Fruits were slides and dipped in the Alg/HNTs-CDs/E mixture for 2 min, and then the excess coating materials were left to drip off. The coated fruits were stored at room temperature for 1 week under a relative humidity (RH) of 60 %. Uncoated fruits were used as controls and were stored under the same conditions.

The statistical analysis of the experimental data was estimated using the statistical analysis software Past 4.03. All tests were performed in duplicate or triplicate (see infra), and the data were analyzed using a one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA). The significant difference values were tested using the Tukey test with a 95 % of confidence interval (p < 0.05). The final results were displayed in the form of mean ± standard deviation.

In summary, in the present manuscript we report the development and characterization of functional film based on alginate filled with modified halloysite nanotubes (HNTs) as an active coating for food packaging applications.

Firstly, the covalent modification of the halloysite external surface was performed by introducing carbon dots (CDs), which confer antioxidant properties to the clay, as proved by DPPH measurements. The modified HNTs were then used as filler in the alginate matrix to obtain the final film (Alg/HNTs-CDs), which was characterized using various physico-chemical techniques. Mechanical analyses before and after film exposure to UV light showed that the stress at break remains unaltered for Alg/HNTs-CDs, highlighting that alginate is fully protected by the addition of HNTs-CDs filler. This indicates that the film represents an efficient photo-protective barrier, preserving the structure of the alginate matrix from UV exposure. The UV barrier properties can be attributed to the presence of CDs on the HNTs surface, which exhibit good absorption features. AFM measurements showed that CDs are clearly visible at the nanocomposite surface, indicating a segregation process at the interface, which could be beneficial for the observed UV light protection.

The introduction of HNTs-CDs filler into the alginate matrix also improved water barrier permeability and decreased the moisture content of the final film. Furthermore, antioxidant measurements showed that after the introduction of HNTs-CDs into alginate, the film retains its antioxidant properties.

To fully exploit the potential of HNTs, the filler was also loaded with a natural extract from M. cisplatensis leaves, which has shown promising antimicrobial activity. Kinetic release experiments from both the filler and the film showed that the latter ensures a slow and sustained release of the active ingredient over time in food simulant D1, providing sustained long antimicrobial protection. Finally, preliminary studies of the effect of Alg/HNTs-CDs/E nanocomposite film on coated and uncoated fruits were carried out on apple and banana slices. These experiments showed that coating with Alg/HNTs-CDs/E nanocomposite film prevents oxidation and extends the storage time of the fruits.

In conclusion, the combination of alginate biopolymer with HNTs-CDs filler and the M. cisplatensis extract provides an efficient UV barrier, prevents oxidation, and offers antimicrobial protection, thereby extending the shelf-life of fruits. Finally, due to the biocompatibility of HNTs and low migration of the extract from the film, the proposed nanocomposite could be considered a safe material for food coating.

Marina Massaro: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Giuseppe Lazzara: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Rita Sánchez-Espejo: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis. César Viseras Iborra: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis. Brigida D'Abrosca: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis. Antonio Fiorentino: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis. Grazia M.L. Messina: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis. Serena Riela: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.
